Static infographics:
two ways to build them.

Both approaches produce the same finished post at 1080×1350. They differ entirely in who does the layout — and that one difference decides how fast we can change a number, and how good the poster looks.

Internal review · 8 July 2026
Approach 1

Generate the image. Claude designs on top.

The image model produces only the photograph — the chai glass, the stack of objects. Claude then builds the headline, the stat cards, the charts and the leader lines in code, and lays them over the photo. Every number on the poster is live text, not pixels.

Pros

  • Full control over content changes. Claude owns every word and number, so a correction is a text edit.
  • Changing a figure is instant and free — no re-render, no credits, and the rest of the poster does not move.
  • Charts are drawn to the real values, so a bar is never the wrong length.
  • The lockup, the source line and the disclaimer are exact, every time.

Cons

  • The infographic never quite settles together. The cards read as a separate layer sitting on top of a photo — because that is exactly what they are.
  • The photo and the data were designed apart, so the composition fights itself.
  • Leader lines have to be hand-aimed at objects in the image, and drift whenever the image is regenerated.
  • It looks like a template. Which is the thing we were trying to escape.
Approach 2

Generate the whole infographic.

The image model composes the entire poster in one pass — hero, headline, charts, leader lines, labels. We reserve an empty band at the top and bottom in the prompt, and drop in only the ONESTOP lockup and the source & disclaimer line afterwards. Tested on both OpenAI's image model and Google's nano banana pro.

Pros

  • It simply looks beautiful. One designer made the whole page, so the type, the object and the data sit together.
  • The scene and the data are composed as one idea — a glass cross-sectioned into its cost layers, a till receipt whose prices are revenues.
  • Reaches visuals no photographer could shoot and no template could hold.
  • Faster to a first draft: one prompt, roughly two minutes.

Cons

  • Any change — even one digit — means regenerating the whole image. More time, more credits.
  • And the regenerated poster comes back with a different composition. You cannot fix one number and keep the rest.
  • Every render must be proof-read end to end. The words are pixels, not text.
  • The model will not reliably print a source line or “Not investment advice.” Those stay ours.
Side by side

Which one, when.

Approach 1 — image + coded design Approach 2 — fully generated
How it looksCards stuck on a photoOne integrated poster
Fixing a numberText edit. Free, instant.Re-render the whole poster. Credits and minutes — and a new composition.
ChartsDrawn to the real valuesCorrect with Google’s model. OpenAI’s drew bars that did not match their values.
SpellingGuaranteedFlawless with Google’s model. OpenAI’s printed “useed” and welded two words together.
Proof-readingOnceEvery single render
Time to first draftSlower — layout is hand-built~2 minutes
Lockup & disclaimerNativeNative (dropped into a reserved band)

The recommendation

Use Approach 2 for the finished post. It is the only one that looks like the StockGro references, and looking right is the entire point of a saveable infographic.

Lock the copy and the numbers before the poster is generated, not after. The real cost of Approach 2 is not the credits — it is that a late correction throws away the composition we approved.

Keep Approach 1 for posts with many numbers that are still moving, or when the same template must be reused weekly with fresh data.

On the numbers

What we checked, and what we dropped.

Three widely-repeated claims that did not survive

“Dixon makes 1 in 3 smartphones in India.” Counterpoint Research’s figure is 19% of India’s 2025 smartphone production — about 1 in 5. The 28% often attributed to Dixon is the combined share of all Indian contract manufacturers. The posters say “made in India,” never “sold in India.”

“Nilkamal is the world’s largest moulded-furniture maker.” That line appears on Nilkamal’s own website but not in its annual report, with no date or method. We dropped the company and used Vinati Organics instead, whose 65% global share of IBB is stated in its audited annual report.

“Indians drink 1 billion cups of chai a day.” The source is a paper whose own abstract contradicts the number. Derived from Tea Board consumption data the honest range is 1.3–1.8 billion — and the poster labels it as our calculation, not a published figure.

Sources on the posters

Chai Economics. Tea Board of India; Department of Consumer Affairs all-India prices (8 July 2026); IOCL commercial LPG rates. The cost split and cups-per-day are OneStop calculations from those inputs, and are labelled as such.

Invisible Giants. FY26 revenue from operations per each company’s exchange filings. Dixon’s production share: Counterpoint Research, April 2026. EPL’s “1 in 3 oral-care tubes” is the company’s own stated figure and is attributed as such.

Education only. Not investment advice. No recommendation to buy or sell any security.
Internal review page — not for distribution outside the team.
